mappings.rationaleAI: Britain's geological formation over 3 billion years covering Precambrian rocks, Carboniferous coal measures, Mesozoic chalk, volcanic activity, and sedimentary processes comprehensively addresses igneous (B.1), sedimentary (B.2), and metamorphic rocks (B.3), rock associations (B.4), and fossil fuels (B.5).
The Earth's core composition (iron and nickel) and core-mantle interactions address the occurrence of elements in the core (C.1) and mantle (C.2).
Earth's formation and differentiation into core, mantle, and crust addresses the distribution of elements across Earth's layers (C.1-C.3).
The geological timescale and stratigraphic principles address the distribution of sedimentary rocks through time (B.2.f).

Plate tectonics covering subduction, mountain building, volcanic activity, and seafloor spreading addresses formation of igneous rocks from magma (B.1.c) and rock associations in different crustal environments (B.4).
Vulcanology covering magma chemistry, eruption styles, and volcano types addresses formation of igneous rocks (B.1.c) and extrusive and pyroclastic igneous rocks (B.1.e.ii, B.1.e.iii).
The Mariana Trench formed by Pacific Plate subduction beneath the Mariana Plate addresses rock associations in oceanic regions (B.4.a).
Carbon's role in fossil fuels and the carbon cycle addresses fossil fuels (B.5) and selective concentration of elements by organisms in the biosphere (C.6).

Early geology covering stratigraphy, identification and dating of geological strata using fossils, and debates between neptunists and plutonists addresses rocks of the Earth's crust (B) and distribution of sedimentary rocks through time (B.2.f).
Fossils' significance for stratigraphy and dating geological strata addresses the distribution of sedimentary rocks through time (B.2.f) and organic-rich sedimentary deposits (B.5).
Crystallography determining atomic arrangements within crystalline solids addresses the internal structure and morphology of minerals (A.1) and their physical properties (A.2).
